- Caller sends request to a stable DNS name (e.g., `payment-service.prod.svc.cluster.local`).
- K8s kube-proxy or a service mesh (Istio, Linkerd) handles routing to healthy pods.
- Pro: Services don't need discovery logic. Con: Extra hop through proxy.

In Kubernetes:
- A `Service` object creates a stable virtual IP (ClusterIP) and DNS name.
- kube-proxy routes traffic to pods matching the selector.
- Pods come and go — the Service IP stays constant.

Service mesh (Istio):
- Adds mTLS between services, observability, retries, circuit breaking.
- Each pod gets a sidecar proxy (Envoy) that handles all inter-service traffic.

── In the Wild ──
Netflix built Eureka (open-sourced) for service discovery. Kubernetes eliminated the need for external discovery registries for most companies — K8s DNS is now the de facto standard.

title: Why does your website load in 50ms in the US but 3 seconds in India?
description: Your server is in us-east-1. Users in India complain of 3-second load times. Your US users see 50ms. What's the fix?
hints: Are all static assets served through a CDN?
Are cache-control headers set correctly (max-age, s-maxage)?
Is your CDN cache invalidation strategy defined for deployments?
Is your API origin protected (CDN only, not publicly exposed)?
approach: CDN (Content Delivery Network) — cache static assets at edge nodes geographically close to users.
solutionText:
The speed of light is not negotiable. A round trip from Mumbai to Virginia (us-east-1) is physically ~280ms minimum — before any processing. With TCP handshakes, TLS negotiation, and actual response: easily 1-3 seconds.

A CDN fixes this by putting copies of your content on servers (edge nodes / PoPs — Points of Presence) physically close to users.

How it works:
1. User in Mumbai requests `cdn.yourapp.com/logo.png`.
2. DNS resolves to nearest CDN edge (e.g., Mumbai PoP).
3. Edge checks if it has the file cached → HIT: serve instantly (<20ms). MISS: fetch from your origin server, cache it, serve it.
4. Next Mumbai user gets it from edge cache.

What to cache on CDN:
- Static assets: JS, CSS, images, fonts, videos.
- Public API responses that don't change per user.
- HTML for non-personalized pages.

What NOT to cache:
- Authenticated/personalized responses.
- Checkout flows.
- Write operations.

CDN providers: Cloudflare (free tier is excellent), AWS CloudFront, Fastly (used by GitHub), Akamai.

Bonus: CDNs also provide DDoS protection by absorbing traffic at the edge before it reaches your origin.

── In the Wild ──
Cloudflare serves ~20% of the internet's traffic from their edge network. GitHub uses Fastly. Most global SaaS products would be unusable in Asia/Africa/South America without CDN.

title: Your system is down. You have no idea why. How did you get here?
description: Prod is on fire. No alerts fired. No dashboards show anything wrong. Users are reporting errors. You're flying blind. What's missing?
hints: Do you have p95/p99 latency tracked per endpoint?
Are logs structured (JSON) with correlation IDs?
Do you have distributed tracing across service boundaries?
Are alerts based on user-facing symptoms (error rate, latency) not just infra metrics?
approach: You have no observability — no metrics, no structured logs, no distributed traces. The three pillars.
solutionText:
Observability is your ability to understand what's happening inside your system from the outside. It has three pillars:

1. Metrics (the numbers):
- Aggregate numerical measurements over time: request rate, error rate, latency (p50/p95/p99), CPU, memory.
- Tools: Prometheus (scrape) + Grafana (visualize), Datadog, CloudWatch.
- Key: Track the RED method — Rate, Errors, Duration for every service.

2. Logs (the events):
- Structured logs (JSON) with correlation IDs so you can trace a single request across all services.
- Ship to: Elasticsearch (ELK stack), Loki + Grafana, Datadog Logs.
- Anti-pattern: `print('something went wrong')` — tells you nothing useful.

3. Traces (the journey):
- Distributed tracing shows a single request's path through all microservices with timing at each step.
- Identify which service added 2 seconds of latency.
- Tools: Jaeger, Zipkin, Datadog APM, AWS X-Ray. Standard: OpenTelemetry.

Alerts that matter:
- Alert on symptoms, not causes: 'Error rate > 1%' not 'CPU > 80%'.
- Use SLOs (Service Level Objectives): 'p99 latency < 200ms' — alert when breached.
- Avoid alert fatigue: if alerts fire daily and are ignored, they're useless.

Correlation ID pattern: Generate a UUID at the API gateway for each request. Pass it as a header to all downstream services. Log it everywhere. Now you can trace one user's broken request across 10 services.

── In the Wild ──
Google's SRE book introduced the concepts of SLOs, SLIs, and error budgets which are now industry standard. The OpenTelemetry project (CNCF) is becoming the standard observability SDK across languages.

title: Why did changing your API break every client at once?
description: You rename a field in your API response. Your mobile app crashes. Web app breaks. Third-party integrations fail. How do you make API changes safely?
hints: Are breaking API changes versioned?
Is there a deprecation notice period before removing fields?
Do you have contract tests (Pact or similar) between services?
Is your API documented with an OpenAPI spec?
approach: API versioning + backward-compatible changes + deprecation period. Never remove fields without notice.
solutionText:
APIs are contracts. Breaking the contract breaks every client. Rules for safe API evolution:

Backward-compatible changes (safe):
- Adding new optional fields to responses.
- Adding new optional request parameters.
- Adding new endpoints.
- Adding new enum values (careful with exhaustive switches in clients).

Breaking changes (dangerous):
- Renaming or removing fields.
- Changing field types (string → int).
- Changing endpoint URLs.
- Changing authentication method.
- Making optional fields required.

Versioning strategies:
1. URL versioning: `/api/v1/users`, `/api/v2/users`. Most common. Easy to route. Explicit.
2. Header versioning: `Accept: application/vnd.myapi.v2+json`. Clean URLs, but harder to test in browser.
3. Query param: `?version=2`. Simple, widely supported.

Deployment strategy:
1. Never delete — deprecate first. Mark fields as deprecated in docs, add response headers.
2. Run v1 and v2 in parallel for a deprecation window (e.g., 6 months).
3. Notify clients via email/changelog before removing.
4. Use contract testing (Pact) to verify clients and server agree on schema.

Tip: Use an API schema (OpenAPI/Swagger spec) as your single source of truth. Generate client SDKs from it to reduce manual contract drift.

── In the Wild ──
Stripe has maintained backward compatibility for 12+ years. They never remove API fields — they add new ones. Twitter's forced API version upgrade in 2023 destroyed the third-party app ecosystem overnight.

title: Why do you need a queue? Why not just call the other service directly?
description: Service A needs to notify Service B after every user signup. Why use Kafka/RabbitMQ instead of a direct HTTP call?
hints: Are non-critical async operations (emails, notifications) decoupled via queue?
Do you have a Dead Letter Queue for failed messages?
Is message schema versioned so producers and consumers can evolve independently?
Is queue depth monitored and alerting set up for abnormal growth?
approach: Decoupling, durability, and backpressure. If B is down, A still works. Messages wait in the queue.
solutionText:
Direct HTTP call (synchronous) problems:
- If B is down, A's request fails. You either lose the event or need retry logic in A.
- If B is slow, A is slow (tight coupling of latency).
- A needs to know B's address (coupling of location).
- Can't fan-out: if C and D also need signup events, A needs to call all three.

Message Queue (asynchronous) benefits:

1. Durability: Messages are persisted. If B crashes, messages wait in the queue. B catches up when it recovers.

2. Decoupling: A doesn't know or care about B. A just publishes to a topic. Any number of consumers can subscribe.

3. Backpressure: B can consume at its own rate. If B is slow, queue grows — you see it in metrics and scale B independently.

4. Retry with DLQ: Failed message processing goes to a Dead Letter Queue. You investigate and replay, not lose.

5. Fan-out: One signup event → email service, analytics service, CRM service all consume independently.

When NOT to use a queue:
- When B's response is needed before A can continue (payment confirmation).
- When you need strict ordering with transactional guarantees (use DB + Saga instead).

Kafka vs RabbitMQ:
- Kafka: Log-based, messages retained for days/weeks, consumers maintain their own offset. Replay possible. High throughput.
- RabbitMQ: Traditional message broker, push-based, messages deleted on ack. Simpler for task queues.

── In the Wild ──
Uber's dispatch system, LinkedIn's activity feed, and Netflix's event pipeline all rely on Kafka. A simple startup alternative: use Postgres as a queue (Outbox pattern) before introducing Kafka complexity.

title: Why can reading someone else's data be as simple as changing a number in the URL?
description: GET /api/orders/12345 returns your order. GET /api/orders/12344 returns someone else's. Why does this happen and how do you stop it?
hints: Do all resource endpoints verify the requesting user owns or has access to the resource?
Are you using UUIDs instead of sequential integers for public-facing IDs?
Do you have automated tests that verify cross-user resource access returns 403?
Is authorization logic centralized (not duplicated per endpoint)?
approach: IDOR — Insecure Direct Object Reference. Missing authorization check. Always verify the requesting user owns the resource.
solutionText:
IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) is in the OWASP Top 10 — one of the most common and impactful vulnerabilities.

The bug:
```
GET /api/orders/12345
Authorization: Bearer <valid token>
```
Server fetches order 12345 from DB and returns it — without checking if the authenticated user actually owns order 12345.

The attack: attacker increments the ID, enumerates orders, reads all customers' data.

Fixes:

1. Always check ownership server-side:
```sql
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE id = 12345 AND user_id = current_user_id
```
If no result → 404 or 403. Never return a resource without verifying the requesting user is authorized.

2. Use non-sequential IDs: UUIDs (v4) instead of auto-increment integers. Not a security fix on its own, but removes enumeration as an easy attack. An attacker can still test UUIDs from a leaked list.

3. Role-based access control (RBAC): Define explicit permissions. 'User role can only access resources they own. Admin role can access all.'

4. Indirect references: Instead of exposing database IDs, map to user-scoped tokens. Less practical for APIs.

Critical: Authentication (who are you?) ≠ Authorization (what are you allowed to do?). Most IDOR bugs happen because auth (JWT verification) was implemented but authz (ownership check) was skipped.

── In the Wild ──
IDOR vulnerabilities have been found in Facebook (2013), Instagram, Uber, and hundreds of startups. Bug bounty hunters look for this first. It's trivially exploitable and often pays $1000-$10,000 bounties.

title: Why does adding a single cache server invalidate 80% of your cached data?
description: You have 4 Redis nodes. You add a 5th. Suddenly 80% of cache keys map to different nodes — massive cache miss storm. How do you prevent this?
hints: Is your cache cluster using consistent hashing (not modulo N)?
Have you tested adding/removing a cache node and measured cache miss rate?
Are virtual nodes enabled to ensure even data distribution?
Is your rebalancing process automated and monitored?
approach: Consistent hashing — only ~1/N of keys remaps when adding/removing a node, instead of all of them.
solutionText:
Naive sharding: key assignment = hash(key) % N (number of nodes).
- 4 nodes: key 'user:123' → hash % 4 → node 2
- 5 nodes: key 'user:123' → hash % 5 → node 3 (different!)
- Adding one node remaps most keys to different nodes. Everything is a cache miss.

Consistent Hashing solution:

1. Imagine a hash ring from 0 to 2^32.
2. Place each node at a position on the ring based on hash(node_id).
3. For each key: hash(key) → position → walk clockwise to nearest node.
4. Adding a new node: only keys between the new node and its predecessor need to move. ~1/N of keys.
5. Removing a node: only its keys move to the next node. ~1/N of keys.

Virtual nodes: Instead of 1 point per node on the ring, use 100-200 virtual points per node. This ensures even distribution even with few nodes and different node capacities.

Used by: Amazon DynamoDB, Apache Cassandra, Memcached (with ketama algorithm), Riak, and most distributed caches.

Bonus concept: Rendezvous hashing (HRW) — alternative to consistent hashing with similar properties, simpler implementation.

── In the Wild ──
Cassandra uses consistent hashing + virtual nodes (vnodes) as its core data distribution mechanism. The original Dynamo paper (Amazon, 2007) introduced consistent hashing at scale to the industry.

title: Why is your read query fast but writing to the same table is destroying performance?
description: You have an index on user_id. Reads are instant. But INSERT performance has degraded 10x since the table grew. Why?
hints: Have you audited indexes for usage with pg_stat_user_indexes or similar?
Are there unused indexes that can be dropped?
Are you using partial indexes for common filtered queries?
Is AUTOVACUUM (Postgres) or equivalent running regularly to reclaim dead index entries?
approach: Every index you add speeds up reads but slows down writes — each INSERT must update all indexes on the table.
solutionText:
An index is a separate data structure (typically a B-tree) that the database maintains in addition to the main table. It allows O(log n) lookups instead of O(n) full scans.

But maintaining an index has a write cost:
- On every INSERT: the new row's values must be inserted into every applicable index.
- On every UPDATE: changed columns must be updated in their indexes.
- On every DELETE: the row must be removed from all indexes.

If you have 8 indexes on a heavily written table, every INSERT does 9 writes (1 row + 8 index updates). With millions of inserts/hour, this becomes significant.

Additionally:
- B-tree rebalancing during high insert rates causes page splits and fragmentation.
- Index bloat: dead rows from UPDATEs leave stale index entries (especially in Postgres — VACUUM reclaims them).
- Covering indexes grow large and take more disk I/O to update.

Balancing strategies:
1. Audit your indexes. Many tables accumulate unused indexes from old queries that no longer run.
2. Use `pg_stat_user_indexes` (Postgres) to find indexes with 0 scans.
3. Drop unused indexes. This is a free write performance win.
4. Partial indexes: Index only the rows you actually query. `CREATE INDEX ON orders(status) WHERE status = 'pending'` — far smaller than a full index.
5. For heavy write workloads: consider write-optimized storage (LSM trees) — Cassandra, RocksDB, InfluxDB use these instead of B-trees.

── In the Wild ──
Cloudflare published a post on dropping unused Postgres indexes that gave them a significant write throughput improvement. Index auditing is one of the fastest free DB performance wins.
